message: Node started: Algorithmic Blueprint: Tree Merging Transformation 1. State Representation: Represent the initial tree and final tree using adjacency lists or parent arrays. For each node, identify its parent and its set of children. Key Invariant: In the final tree, every node u must correspond to a set of nodes in the initial tree that were merged together. The node u itself must be the maximum value among all nodes in its initial-tree equivalence class. 2. Transformation Strategy (Bottom-Up Merging): The transformation must proceed from the leaves up to the root to ensure that when merging siblings, their children have already been processed/merged into the correct final structure. For each node u in the final tree: a. Identify the set of children of u in the final tree: {c1, c2, ..., ck}. b. Identify the set of children of u in the initial tree: {s1, s2, ..., sm}. c. Note that every child ci in the final tree must be represented by some node in the initial tree that is a descendant of u. Specifically, each ci corresponds to a unique node in the initial tree that is a child of u or a descendant of a child of u that eventually merges into ci. d. However, the problem simplifies this: a merge operation only combines siblings. Therefore, for a node u to have children {c1, ..., ck} in the final tree, all initial children of u that are NOT part of the set {c1, ..., ck} must be merged into one of the ci nodes. e. More accurately: For a node u, let its initial children be S. Let its final children be F. Every node in F must be a descendant of some node in S. Because we only merge siblings, each node in F must be exactly one of the nodes in S, or a node that was formed by merging nodes in S. But the rule says merged node keeps the larger value. This implies that for any node u, its children in the final tree must be a subset of its children in the initial tree, OR the children in the final tree are the result of merging some of the initial children. f. Correct Logic: For each node u (traversed post-order/bottom-up): Let the initial children of u be children_init(u). Let the final children of u be children_final(u). For each child v in children_final(u), it must be that v is an initial child of u. Refined Logic: For each node u, identify which of its initial children are NOT present in its final children list.
